title: Five-Minute Gut Check Meditation
subtitle: A short body-based meditation for accessing your own knowing
excerpt: A five-minute body-based meditation for accessing inner knowing before a decision or at the start of the day. The practice asks the body the question rather than the analytical mind, and notices what comes back.
sourceNotes: Draws on interoception and body-scan practices from mindfulness and somatic traditions.

practice.whenToUse: Use before a decision, at the start of the day, or any time you want to consult something other than the analytical mind. Good when you already know the answer but cannot hear it clearly.

content.text: A five-minute meditation for accessing what your body already knows. The analytical mind generates options and arguments. The gut check goes below them to the felt sense: what the body says yes or no to before the reasoning starts.
New to meditation as a practice? Read
Sit comfortably, both feet on the floor. Close your eyes.
Take three slow breaths, breathing out longer than you breathe in. Let the body settle.
Bring your attention to your chest and abdomen. Not thinking about them. Just placing attention there.
Hold the question, situation, or decision you want to consult your body about. Do not think through it. Simply hold it while keeping attention in the body.
Notice: is there expansion or contraction? Openness or tightening? A shift toward or away? Do not interpret immediately. Stay with the physical sensation for a minute.
Open your eyes. Note what the body said before the mind starts to argue with it.
The body signal is not always right. But it is separate from the reasoning mind. For many decisions it is more reliable than any argument that can be made for either side.
Draws on body-scan and mindfulness practices.
